items.myth: Unit tests prove the code is correct.
items.reality: Tests prove the code does what the tests *say* it should do. They can't prove there are no bugs you didn't think of. Tests are a strong line of defence, not a correctness guarantee.
items.example: A chunker with 100% coverage can still produce overlapping chunks if you forgot to test for that property.

items.myth: 100% coverage means the code is well-tested.
items.reality: Coverage measures *execution*, not *assertion*. You can have 100% coverage with `assert True` everywhere. Look at branch coverage on critical paths, and judge tests by what they'd catch when broken.
items.example: Replace your asserts with `assert True` and rerun coverage — still 100%. The number didn't change; the protection vanished.

items.myth: If it has tests, it's testable code.
items.reality: Code with bad design can be made testable by piling on mocks — but the tests will be fragile and verbose. Testable design has few dependencies, pure functions where possible, and inverted I/O.
items.example: 10 lines of code with 50 lines of mock setup is a design smell — the code is over-coupled.

items.myth: You should write tests after the code is working.
items.reality: Tests-first (TDD) often produces better-designed code because you confront the API before committing to the implementation. Tests-after still beats no tests — but the design feedback is gone.
items.example: Writing the test first forces you to ask 'how does my caller want to use this?' before 'how do I implement it?'.

items.reality: Unit tests catch *unit-scope* bugs. Integration bugs (wiring, contracts, environment) need integration tests; user-visible bugs need E2E tests; semantic correctness of an LLM needs evals. Different layers, different tests.
items.example: Each function's unit tests can pass while a refactored interface breaks every caller — only integration tests catch that.
